summary: Shirk is the association of partners, peers, or rivals with God (Allah) in His lordship, worship, or attributes. In Islamic theology it is the conceptual and moral opposite of tawhid, the affirmation of divine oneness.
description:
The term shirk derives from an Arabic root meaning "to share" or "to make a partner," and denotes ascribing a sharer (sharīk) to God in that which is proper to Him alone. The Qur'an returns to the theme repeatedly, insisting that God has no partner and that He does not share His powers or the worship due to Him; several verses (notably Q 4:48 and 4:116) present shirk as the one sin God will not forgive if a person dies without repenting of it, while forgiving lesser sins at His discretion.

In the dogmatic vocabulary that developed in the classical period, shirk functions as the antonym of tawhid, and much of the doctrine of divine oneness is articulated by negating shirk. Later theological and legal literature commonly distinguished degrees and kinds of shirk, for example a "greater" shirk (al-shirk al-akbar), understood as open association such as directing worship or supplication to another besides God, and a "lesser" or "hidden" shirk (al-shirk al-asghar), covering acts such as performing devotion for the sake of human recognition. Some traditions map these onto the same dimensions used to analyze tawhid, lordship (rubūbiyya), divinity and worship (ulūhiyya), and the divine names and attributes (al-asmāʾ wa-l-ṣifāt).

The precise boundary of what counts as shirk, and whether particular practices fall under it, has been contested among schools and jurists across Islamic history; this entry describes the concept without adjudicating those disputes.
editorial_notes: Scope is deliberately introductory. The greater/lesser (akbar/asghar) taxonomy and the mapping onto rubūbiyya/ulūhiyya/al-asmāʾ-wa-l-ṣifāt are widely attested in classical and later literature but were systematized over time and are stated here descriptively, not as a single agreed schema. The question of which specific practices constitute shirk has been a live and sometimes polemical dispute among schools and movements; per the neutrality policy those disputes are named but not adjudicated, and contemporary sectarian applications of the term are treated as out of scope. Qur'anic references (Q 4:48, 4:116) are cited via the secondary summaries consulted rather than an independent text-critical reading, so a scholar reviewing this entry should verify them against the Qur'an directly. A Muslim/Islamic scholarly source has been added for triangulation (neutrality principle #8): al-Shahrastānī's classical heresiography Kitāb al-Milal wa-l-Niḥal, cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line.

relations.note: Shirk is the direct negation of the oneness affirmed by tawhid.
One analyzed dimension is shirk with respect to the divine names and attributes.
Rejection of shirk is bound up with affirming God's incomparability.

historical_period.note: A central Qur'anic theme from the earliest revelation; elaborated into a technical typology by theologians and jurists in the following centuries.

sources.citation: Gimaret, D. "Sh̲irk." In Encyclopaedia of Islam, 2nd ed. Leiden: Brill.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; text not reproduced.

sources.citation: "Shirk (Islam)." Wikipedia. Encyclopedic overview citing Encyclopaedia of Islam, 2nd ed. for the greater/lesser typology and Q 4:48, 4:116.
Winter, T. (ed.), The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology. Cambridge University Press, 2008.

al-Shahrastānī, Muḥammad ibn ʿAbd al-Karīm. Kitāb al-Milal wa-l-Niḥal. Partial English trans. A. K. Kazi and J. G. Flynn, Muslim Sects and Divisions, Kegan Paul International, 1984. Classical Muslim heresiography surveying the sects and their doctrines on divine oneness and its associationist opposites (dualists, associators).
sources.license: Proprietary translation; classical Arabic text public domain. Cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
